sentences.text: Yes.
I was born in Stuttgart, Germany, southern part of Germany, on September 9th, 1916.
And I went to school there, elementary school, then high school.
Yes.
All right.
My parents Walter and Tilly, or Mathilde Liebmann.
My father was a lawyer and had a doctorate, for which he had to write a dissertation at that time.
It was not like doctor juries here in this country.
And my mother was essentially a homemaker, although she had studied design in Vienna.
And that allowed her to design and make all our clothes, my sister and my clothes.
And I have an older sister named Ruth Marianne, Marianne.
And she was born before World War I.
I was born during the war, middle of the war.
My father was an officer in the German army.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] That's my phone.
Excuse me.
And so, as I said, she was four years older.

And we lived-- during the war, those war years, until the war ended in 1918, we lived with my grandparents, as did my cousins and their parents, their mothers-- I should say their mothers, because all the men in our family were in the war serving the German army.
And, as an interesting by product of what I'm saying, my father was originally-- volunteered for the war.
And because he was Jewish-- anyone with a doctorate, or with his education, became an officer, was granted officer status immediately.
He was not because he was Jewish.
And he grew a beard in protest.
I have a picture of him with a beard, in protest.
And he was promoted to officer status, which I still wonder that they allowed him to do that.
Yes, he grew a beard.
You weren't supposed to have a beard in the army, you know.
And so he did.
And I have a picture of that.
And then, he was promoted, which  he should have been to begin with.
Anyway, he was there until the end of the war, on November 18.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They told me.
Naturally, I did not know him when he came back.
I was only two years old and apparently did not recognize him as my father, wouldn't go to him, initially.
But anyway, we-- he had a thriving practice, a law practice.
Build it up again after the war and had two secretaries, as I recall.
He was by himself.
And we bought a house.
We lived in an apartment and then bought a house in 1927, which my father told us he bought with the money he saved from giving up smoking.
Because he didn't want us to start smoking.
So anyway, we went to-- the school system is different in Germany as it is in the United States.
High school starts in the fifth grade.
And I was allowed to skip the fourth grade and go directly from grade school to high school, the [NON-ENGLISH].
It was named after a queen, whom I-- I don't remember when she lived, I think in the 19th century some time.
And it was a gymnasium, which means it was led all the way to the end exam which is the abitur, A-B-I-T-U-R, or maturum.
And I had to take two street cars to go there at the age of nine, but I managed.
Seemed to manage pretty well.
So I was-- we had a lot of culture in the house.
We had evenings, music evenings.
I learned to play the violin from the time I was about eight years old.
Both my parents played piano.
My sister played piano.
We had people, other people, who participated in the music evenings, friends of my parents.
We had reading evenings, [GERMAN], they are called in German, where my parents had friends in, and they were reading a book, or had been reading, and then discussing it.
I was usually not included that.
Those were evening activities.
But I was allowed to go to the children's opera on Sunday afternoon.
I had season tickets for that.
And- I was-- I was reading a lot.
As a matter of fact, I read all of Grimm's and Andersen's fairy tales before I ever went to school.
I had read them all.

And my father, early on, told me to concentrate my reading on nonfiction because there wasn't enough time in life to read all the fiction there was plus all the nonfiction, science, and history, and so on.
And I still read mostly nonfiction.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Yehudi Menuhin, who is exactly the same age I am.
He's living-- he's 79, as I am.
And he's living in England, has a school where he teaches.
For many years, he's had a music school.
He was one of the most famous, at that time, in the '20s, violinists.
He was a child prodigy, actually.
And when he came to our home town, to my home town, Stuttgart, when he was about eight or nine years old, same age, as I said, I was same age he was, my parents took me to hear him.
Yehudi Menuhin was in knee pants, playing as beautifully as he did when he was an adult later, and I had heard him as an adult.
And I, at that time, didn't realize that he had a great talent.
I thought if I practiced very hard, before I went to school sometimes, or after, that I could play like he did.
But I found out I couldn't.
I didn't have his talent.
It took me a while to find out.
Yes.
Stuttgart is a very attractive city.
It is, again, since I was back, last, in '89, when I was invited back.
It is a town that is-- has a valley and hills surrounding it.
And we used to live in what here would be called a suburb.
But it was part of Stuttgart.
On a hill, one of the hills.
And the school and the stores were in the valley.
And as I said, it has become, again, a very attractive city.
And, sometimes, I walked with friends in the other direction, that is not towards the valley, but away from it, where we, for instance, played tennis.
We would walk through the woods at that time.
The woods were perfectly safe.
And you could-- even children could walk without being endangered.
I liked school very much.
I was-- I liked studying.
I would have stayed home and studied instead of going out if my parents hadn't seen to it that I had to take gymnastics and all kinds of things.
And we had to get out, rain or shine.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Did you record oops?
Not as strict as some of the parents of my friends, my Christian friends.
I had both Lutheran and Catholic friends, mostly Lutheran.
In Stuttgart, they were mostly Protestant.
My mother was very open to having friends in any time we wanted, pretty much.
Some of my friends' parents did not allow that.
You know, you had to sort of make an appointment.
And that's why we had a lot of-- I had a lot of friends in our house.
I had friends, two girls next door to me, one was in my class, and the other one was somewhat younger, where you had to take your shoes off when you came in the house.
And you had to put on their slippers to not ruin their floors.
My mother was not like that.
And, however, as far as money, we had to save money, for instance.

If we wanted something, like my first camera, my sister's first bicycle, we saved up for maybe two years money that we earned by-- I used to draw and paint, and do things, or do things for people, or crochet something, when I was old enough.
And we got money for birthdays and Christmas, and saved for-- I saved for my first watch.
My cousins who were all brought up as Lutherans, as Christians, they had confirmation.
And they got nice things that way.
And I felt bad that we didn't get that.
We didn't observe-- we didn't observe any Jewish rituals.
And we didn't observe any Christian-- well, we did have Christmas.
We didn't observe Jewish holidays.
My grandmother on my father's side fasted on Yom Kippur and the Day of Atonement.
And my grandfather, on my mother's side, also observed.
And once or twice, I was allowed to go with him to the temple.
This was a reform temple in Stuttgart.
But, generally, we had Christmas, because we had, at that time, maids.
Almost everybody we knew had a maid.
They came from the country.
And they got their dowries from their employers.
And so you celebrated Christmas.
I always felt that they got more gifts than we did, the maids, because they did, they got, I guess their whole dowry on various Christmases.
And we had a Christmas dinner, duck, or the goose, or whatever.
That was long before I became a vegetarian.
And so we did.
I have good memories of Christmas Eve, particularly.
And I celebrated for a long time here.
I had people, a party on Christmas Eve, and had a lot of people in during my younger years.
And we had New Year's.
But we did not, as I said, at home, observe the holidays.
At that time, initially, until about 1932, I had only non-Jewish friends.
All my girlfriends were Christians, As I said, mostly Lutheran in that area, in southern Germany.
In Bavaria, it was more Catholic.
But [GERMAN], where I lived, was more Protestant.
And, I, once in a while, went to church with them.
But, generally, since I didn't have any religious instruction, I didn't have a problem until Hitler came into power-- even before Hitler, when all my teachers had become Nazis.
And it became more obvious to me that, you know, I didn't know I was going to lose all my non-Jewish friends until it actually happened.
It's in my article on art class, where I describe what happened to me there.
That was in 1932, before Hitler came to power.
We had an assignment, a stained glass window.
And I had good grades in art.
And I liked it.
I still like it.
And I still have done some recent painting.

When he-- the professor who critiqued our paintings came around and saw that I had painted Christ on the cross, in a-- within the context of a stained glass window, and he yelled at me and said, as a Jew, you have no right to paint Christ on the cross, and then became so enraged that he reached back, went to his desk, and threw the ink well all across the room, so that the ink was flowing down the cabinet and on the other end of the room.
And that's how enraged he was that I had the nerve to do that.
But my best friend, and I wrote about her, was my friend since grade school.
We were for about eight years by the time Hitler came to power, and who then stopped talking to me, just almost abruptly.
She was, as I said, for eight years, my best friend.
I had another one whom I had known for about four years.
And she also stopped talking to me.
And then I described how my friend-- Helga, I call her.
It was not her real name.
When she-- one day, after she had stopped talking to me in school, when she asked me to meet her in the woods.
Stuttgart was surrounded by woods.
On the hills, there were woods, wooded areas.
And she asked me to go right to the end station of that street car number 7.
And she would meet me there in the woods.
There was also a zoo there that we used to go to quite often.
Anyway, I did do that.
We did not ride together on the same street car.
We rode on separate street cars.
And she called me into the woods.
And she waved to me and asked me to come further into the woods.
And she said, you know, I can't come to your house anymore, or you to mine.
But we could meet here once in a while.
And I said, no, we couldn't.
Because we would be found out.
Both of us would be in trouble.
If we can't talk normally the way we used to, then we just can't see each other anymore.
And that was the last I heard of her.
I went back by myself on the street car.
And I guess she went on another street car home.
And that was-- because I had had no Jewish friends-- I was the only Jewish girl in the class.
Because I had no religious instruction, it was customary for the ministers, priests, and rabbis to come to the schools at the same time and give religious instruction to the students.
And since I didn't have any, I was allowed-- well, they told me to sit and do my homework while my Lutheran friends had their instruction.
Because I listened in on it, I didn't really do my homework.
It would be a little hard to do that while somebody else is talking.

And so then, in 1933, I decided, since my friends, this friend and then this other friend I had had for about four years-- and we had been pretty close for those four years, too-- that I needed to have some Jewish friends.
And that's when I joined this German Jewish youth organization, which was very patriotic German.
We wanted to stay in Germany, actually.
And we thought there would be a new Germany, and it would all change again.
It was called Bund deutsch-judischer Jugend.
That's a club of German Jewish youth.
And it had another name, the Ring, R-I-N-G, because a ring is round.
And so we got together, I think, every week.
And I also joined a dancing-- my parents insisted that I go to dancing class, which was also Jewish, at that time to meet-- so I met some boys there, too.
They were also in another youth group.
And that was before I left-- before I left for England in 1934.
I didn't have my other friends anymore.
And there was some truth to that.
There was some reality to that, that my friend Helga said to me in the woods.
She said, my father will lose his job if you come to my house or I to yours.
And probably, she was right.
But it was hard for me to understand and accept it at the time.
Yes.
That was the group where I went for-- where they asked for any high school students who were interested in psychology to go to this group, psychology meeting with a woman, female, psychoanalyst.
I think she was a Jungian analyst.
And I met him there, this man.
He was the same age, about 16.
And we became good friends.
He was very much in love with me, probably more so than I was with him.
And he came to the house.
I went to his house once in a while.
They lived in a-- in a suburb further away.
And then his mother, it's in my diary.
His mother came to the house, and talked to my mother, and said, she did not want me to see her son anymore.
That it would be dangerous for him, and for them, for his parents, and so on.
So that was the end of that.
Then I met a Jewish boyfriend, a Jewish friend in the dancing class.
And he also was in the-- bund, in that ring of German Jewish youth.
And we went on hikes and things like that with other-- with groups and so on.
I saw him later, in Chicago, but he had married by then.
And I still, incidentally, see his widow.
He died a few years ago.
So that was a change.
And the schools, all my teachers had become Nazis.
And we came in with a Hitler salute into the classroom.
And it was very obvious because one of the teachers, a social science teacher, assigned the Communist Party to me.
He asked-- this was, at that time, a girls school.
They are all coed now  in Germany.
But, at that time, it was only girls.
And he asked me, one of 37 students in the class, to study the Communist Party.
There were about just as many parties, pre-Hitler.
People are not aware of that.
There were about 37 different parties in Germany, small-- some were small labor parties, whatever.
And he assigned the Communist Party to me.
And when I came home and told my parents, my father immediately wanted to go to school and object because he knew that he had assigned that to me because, being Jewish, I had to be a communist.
Of course, I explained to kids when I talked in school that, in Germany, all Jews were communists and capitalists at the same time-- which is, of course, impossible.
But this is the way it was portrayed.
They had all the money, presumably, which, of course, wasn't true.
And they also were communists.
They believed in socialism.
Some did.
People believed in socialism, which is different from communism.

But anyway, I became very much interested in Karl Marx while I was studying it in order to give the report, and felt that the premise to each according to his ability, you know, and to each according to his needs, that that was, I felt, a pretty sound theory.
But it was very deliberate that it was assigned, that the teacher assigned it to me.
--associated with communism.
Yeah.
Well, first of all, one of my teachers, a woman teacher-- as I said, we called-- most of our teachers were called professors in high school.
And she taught literature, German literature.
This is in my diary.
She told us not to read the books that were written contemporary-- by contemporary writers because they were all under the Jewish influence.
Even if the authors were not Jewish, they were written under the influence of Jews.
That was her theory.
And I realized how prejudiced she was.
And she also-- my grades went down.
They had been pretty good, generally, also in German.
And we had also English and French, incidentally.
And I realized that the teachers, all the teachers I had, had become Nazis.
Maybe they were Nazis before.
Maybe they were-- it was ingrained in them, you know, the anti-Semitism.
There was a lot of it in Germany.
But everybody had assumed that, after World War I, when so many Jewish men fought in the German army, that this would no longer be the case.
But it, apparently, it was latent in Germany.
And it still is, I believe.
And so, then, of course, later, in '34, I was, you know, told that I was no longer wanted in school, actually.
And that's when I, you know, felt-- and I had been ostracized by everybody.
I felt I really couldn't go anymore anyway.
That's when my father said that he was a veteran, and I had a right to go to school.
But it was not-- didn't help.
And I remember one or two incidents when we, in the beginning, in '33, when the youth leader, his name was Baldur von Schirach, Hitler's youth leader-- came to town to talk to the students.
And we all-- all the schools, I guess, from maybe high school on, from the the age of 9 or 10 on, had to march downtown to meet-- to listen to this leader, youth later, to him talk.
And there was a real conflict because everybody had to use-- were supposed to used the Hitler salute, Heil Hitler.
And then, some decree came out that Jewish children were not allowed to do the Hitler salute.
